topic: ReAct Agent Pattern: The Complete Developer Implementation Guide for 2026

key_points: Step-by-step from-scratch Python implementation of the ReAct loop
Covers Thought→Action→Observation cycle with code snippets
Shows how to handle tool execution and inject observations back into context
Authoritative IBM overview of the ReAct paradigm
Explains error recovery: agent can reason about failures and try alternate approaches
Positions ReAct as the most important single-agent pattern (Yao et al., 2022)
Side-by-side comparison of CoT vs ReAct architecturally
CoT is a single-call technique; ReAct is a multi-turn architectural pattern
ReAct achieves 91% accuracy vs CoT's 87% on multi-step reasoning benchmarks
Recommends ReAct for multi-hop tasks requiring real-time data or multiple systems
Best practices: avoid hard-coding logic, use plug-and-play AI modules
Pitfalls: doing too much upfront leads to unstable/hard-to-debug agents
Failing to integrate feedback mechanisms causes repetitive or erroneous behavior
Transparency advantage: reasoning chain is inspectable for debugging
Places ReAct within the broader landscape of 7 agentic design patterns
Covers ReAct alongside Reflection, Tool Use, Planning, Multi-agent patterns
Shows how ReAct + Reflexion forms the production-grade single-agent stack

stats.fact: ReAct agents achieve 91% accuracy on multi-step reasoning tasks vs Chain-of-Thought's 87%
Gartner forecasts 40% of enterprise applications will embed task-specific AI agents by 2026, up from less than 5% in 2025
Almost 4 in 5 enterprises have adopted AI agents in some form, yet only 1 in 9 runs them in production — a 68-percentage-point deployment gap
LangGraph 0.3+ and OpenAI Agents SDK now support concurrent tool dispatch with per-call timeouts and ordered result collection by default as of 2026
ReAct was first introduced in the Yao et al. 2022 paper and remains the most widely deployed single-agent pattern for interpretability-sensitive applications
ReAct agents are vulnerable to 'Foot-in-the-Door' prompt injection attacks where adversarial observations hijack the reasoning loop

angles: Build a ReAct agent from scratch in pure Python (no frameworks) to show the fundamentals — then graduate to LangGraph's create_react_agent
Explain why ReAct is the 'default' choice for production agents in 2026: interpretability, error recovery, and real-world grounding in a single pattern
Address the 68-point enterprise deployment gap: most companies adopt agents but fail at production — ReAct's observability is the key to closing it
Cover the 'ReAct + Reflexion' production-grade combo — the upgrade path beyond basic ReAct
Tackle common failure modes: infinite loops, tool overload, ambiguous stop conditions — with concrete fixes (max_steps, deduplication, explicit success criteria)
Security angle: ReAct agents face prompt injection via adversarial observations — how to harden your implementation
Framework decision guide: when to use create_react_agent vs. manual StateGraph vs. OpenAI Agents SDK vs. bare Python
keywords: react agent loop thought action observation
react vs chain of thought agent
outline: H2: What Is the ReAct Agent Pattern? (Reasoning + Acting Defined)
H2: How the ReAct Loop Works: Thought → Action → Observation → Repeat
H2: ReAct vs. Chain-of-Thought vs. Plan-and-Execute — Which Pattern to Use
H2: Building a ReAct Agent from Scratch in Python (Zero-Framework)
H2: Using LangGraph's create_react_agent — The 2026 Production Path
H2: Designing Good Tools for ReAct Agents (Naming, Schemas, Error Contracts)
H2: Common Pitfalls and How to Fix Them (Infinite Loops, Latency, Tool Overload)
H2: Security Hardening — Defending Against Prompt Injection in the Observation Loop
H2: Upgrading to ReAct + Reflexion — The Production-Grade Single-Agent Stack
H2: Production Deployment Checklist (Timeouts, Logging, Guardrails, Observability)
